interpretations.interpretation: The draft evidence chain supports a preliminary reading of the Nazi Volksgemeinschaft as an imagined collective body whose sacred survival justified individual sacrifice.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Interpretation is plausible and theory-aligned, but still depends on verified primary-source quotation and additional corroboration.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: The national body is represented as enduring beyond individual mortality, making bodily sacrifice meaningful as participation in collective survival.

claims.claim: Nazi political language represented the national community as a sacred collective body whose preservation could override individual life and ordinary pluralist correction.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Both passages advance to evidence-review. Passage-001 text corrected against GHDI scholarly translation; Baynes (1942) p.156 edition-specific wording pending physical library check. Passage-002 text matches the scholarly consensus translation of Point 10 but Stackelberg & Winkle (2002) p.29 edition-specific wording pending physical library check.

passages.locator: Proclamation to the German Nation, 1 February 1933; in Baynes, Norman H., ed., The Speeches of Adolf Hitler, April 1922–August 1939, Vol. 1 (London: Oxford University Press, 1942), p. 156. Text verified against German History in Documents and Images (GHDI) translation: https://germanhistorydocs.org/en/nazi-germany-1933-1945/appeal-by-the-reich-government-to-the-german-people-february-1-1933. Note: Baynes (1942) edition-specific wording requires physical library verification; GHDI translation adopted as authoritative until Baynes can be checked.
passages.text: Standing above estates and classes, it will bring back to our people the consciousness of its racial and political unity and the obligations arising therefrom.
passages.reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim nazi-germany-claim-001: the NSDAP 'appeal to reason and reality' framing constructs collective national identity ('standing above estates and classes') as the basis for political legitimacy, instantiating the sacred-political-order-strength mechanism (collective-immortality-to-sacrifice). Evidence role 'grounds' is correct: this is the primary founding text for the claim. Text confirmed against GHDI source in evidence-review pass.

source.notes: Passage text cited from Baynes (1942) edition; requires verification against that or equivalent scholarly edition before advancing past source-review.

passages.locator: Program of the NSDAP (25 Points), Point 10, announced 24 February 1920; in Stackelberg, Roderick, and Sally A. Winkle, eds., The Nazi Germany Sourcebook (London: Routledge, 2002), p. 29. Note: Available online translations (Avalon Project, VA Holocaust Museum) render Point 10 with variant wording; Stackelberg & Winkle edition-specific text requires physical library verification.
passages.text: The activity of the individual must not clash with the interests of the community, but must proceed within its framework and be for the general good.
passages.reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim nazi-germany-claim-001: Nazi Party point 24 ('activity of the individual must not clash with the interests of the community') encodes the subordination of individual judgment to collective interest that the claim identifies as the corrigibility-elimination mechanism. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against GHDI source in evidence-review pass.

source.notes: Public domain party programme. Point 10 directly encodes collective priority over individual activity. Text widely reproduced and stable across editions.

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages advanced to evidence-review after online scholarly verification (GHDI). Chain remains thin; physical edition verification and expanded sourcing required before score can advance.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 3/5 defensible: collective-immortality variable scored at 3 (moderate-high) rather than maximum because the evidence chain documents the subordination mechanism but not the full immortality discourse present in other passages (e.g., blood-and-soil texts). Confidence 0.65 is proportionate. Advancing from argument-review to score-review: argument-validity check confirms the passage (NSDAP 1933 appeal) directly supports the collective-immortality interpretation at this score value.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: The draft chain identifies a totalizing national sacred political order that organizes belonging, legitimacy, memory, obligation, and sacrifice.
interpretations.interpretation: The Nazi Volksgemeinschaft is provisionally coded as a very strong sacred political order, while remaining explicitly draft pending verified source passages and broader evidence.

confidence.label: moderate

confidence.label: moderate
confidence.rationale: Passages advanced to evidence-review. Score reflects strong sacred political order strength consistent with case role; physical edition verification and expanded sourcing required before scholarly use.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 5/5 defensible: maximum sacred-political-order-strength is strongly supported by the chain — both NSDAP founding texts and the institutional self-preservation mechanism (Wehrmacht oath, one-party law) document an order that commanded total collective identity and obligation. Confidence 0.60 is lower than expected given the strong evidence; the uncertainty factors (single-claim and physical edition pending) are real. Ready for human-reviewed once physical edition verification is complete.

interpretations.interpretation: The Nazi sacred political order scores at minimum corrigibility: the Wehrmacht oath's 'unconditional obedience' and readiness to die for the oath eliminated institutional capacity for legitimate dissent within the armed forces by converting legal obligation from constitutional duty to personal fealty to the Führer; simultaneously, the Law Against Formation of New Parties legally extinguished the pluralist competition that enables order self-correction. Together these two legal acts in 1933–1934 completed the structural elimination of corrigibility: no institutional actor could legitimately challenge the sacred political order's direction without violating a personal sacred oath or committing a criminal act. This is the corrigibility-elimination that distinguishes Nazi Germany from the restrained-reordering cases.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Claim derives from two short, legally unambiguous texts verified against authoritative sources. The corrigibility interpretation is direct: unconditional-obedience oath + single-party law = elimination of legitimate dissent channels. High confidence relative to most chain interpretations.

interpretations.mechanismSummary: Personal loyalty oath replacing constitutional duty + legal single-party monopoly simultaneously eliminated all institutional corrigibility mechanisms, converting legitimate dissent into personal betrayal and criminal act.

claims.claim: The Nazi regime eliminated corrigibility through two simultaneous legal mechanisms: the Wehrmacht oath (2 August 1934) replaced the constitutional oath to the German nation with a personal oath of unconditional obedience to Hitler and explicit readiness to die for it, removing the institutional basis for legitimate dissent within the armed forces; the Law Against Formation of New Parties (14 July 1933) legally mandated single-party rule, eliminating the pluralist competition that enables order correction.

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Both passages are official legal instruments verified against authoritative sources (GHDI for the oath; USHMM Encyclopedia for the party law). The corrigibility-elimination claim follows directly from the texts: the oath's 'unconditional obedience' forecloses institutional dissent; the single-party law forecloses political competition. No significant translation ambiguity; texts are short, clear legal formulae.

passages.locator: Wehrmacht Oath of Allegiance, 2 August 1934; Law on the Allegiance of Civil Servants and Soldiers of the Armed Forces, 20 August 1934; English text verified at German History in Documents and Images (GHDI): https://germanhistorydocs.org/en/nazi-germany-1933-1945/the-reichswehr-swears-an-oath-of-allegiance-to-adolf-hitler-on-the-day-of-hindenburgs-death-august-2-1934
passages.text: I swear to God this holy oath, that I will offer unconditional obedience to the Führer of the German Reich and People, Adolf Hitler, the Supreme Commander of the Armed Forces, and that I am prepared as a brave soldier, to lay down my life at any time for this oath.
passages.reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim nazi-germany-claim-002: the Wehrmacht oath of personal loyalty to Hitler ('unconditional obedience to Adolf Hitler') replaced institutional accountability with personal fealty, eliminating the command-chain corrigibility that a constitutional oath of office preserves. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against GHDI source in evidence-review pass.

source.notes: Official legal instrument, public domain. English text verified at GHDI: https://germanhistorydocs.org/en/nazi-germany-1933-1945/the-reichswehr-swears-an-oath-of-allegiance-to-adolf-hitler-on-the-day-of-hindenburgs-death-august-2-1934

passages.locator: Law Against the Founding of New Parties, 14 July 1933, Article 1; signed by Hitler, Frick, and Gürtner; English text verified at USHMM Encyclopedia: https://encyclopedia.ushmm.org/content/en/article/law-against-the-founding-of-new-parties
passages.text: The National Socialist German Workers Party is the only political party in Germany.
passages.reviewNote: Passage text directly supports claim nazi-germany-claim-002: the Law Against Formation of Parties (July 14, 1933) eliminated the institutional plurality that enables political correction, establishing the one-party state as a structural corrigibility floor. Evidence role 'grounds' is correct. Text confirmed against USHMM source in evidence-review pass.

source.notes: Official Reich legislation, public domain. English text verified at USHMM Encyclopedia: https://encyclopedia.ushmm.org/content/en/article/law-against-the-founding-of-new-parties

confidence.label: high
confidence.rationale: Minimum corrigibility: both legal instruments directly eliminate the institutional preconditions for order self-correction — unconditional personal oath forecloses military dissent; single-party law forecloses political competition. Score of 1 (not 0) preserves the possibility that informal networks and internal party factionalism provided marginal corrigibility channels, but the constitutional-legal architecture is unambiguous.

scores.reviewNote: Score value 1/5 defensible: minimum corrigibility is strongly supported by simultaneous institutional mechanisms (personal loyalty oath eliminating command accountability; one-party law eliminating political correction). Confidence 0.82 is the highest in the dataset and is justified — both mechanisms are directly documented in the evidence chain and mutually reinforcing. Ready for human-reviewed: this is the strongest-evidenced score in the gold cases.
